sections.title: The Adventure Begins
sections.content_summary: Students join a nature trail at Pachmarhi, Madhya Pradesh — a biosphere reserve — guided by Abha Didi, a nature scientist. The section introduces the concept of responsible behaviour in forests and natural areas. Students are asked to think about dos and don'ts before matching their ideas against a provided set of eight safety rules covering care for animals, plants, and the environment.

tasks.task_text: Can you think of rules that should be followed while you are in a zoo or a forest? For example, (a) We should not feed birds. (b) ……… (c) ……… (d) ………
Why are these rules important to be followed?

sections.title: Animals in the Forest
sections.content_summary: Students observe the rich variety of animals in the Pachmarhi forest and near a pond — including gaur, deer, monkeys, and the Indian Giant Squirrel (a regional endemic). They study birds with special attention to beaks and claws as adaptations for feeding, examining hornbill, peacock, owl, eagle, sunbird, and others. Water-dwelling animals such as fish, turtles, gharial, frogs, and crabs are introduced with their distinctive features and movement. The section also covers leaf diversity — colour, shape, texture, and venation patterns — through collection and rubbing activities.

tasks.task_text: Animals in the Forest — Look closely at the picture and identify the animals. You may take help from your teacher. Divide them into two categories: 1. Animals on land: 2. Birds in the sky:
Name some animals including the birds that you have seen. Write any one feature of each of them.
Compare its features with the squirrels you see in your area.
Look at the footprints and guess the name of the animals that you think must have come to the pond to drink water.
Observe and draw the footprint of any animal in the space given here.
Name the birds shown below.

Take different food items like grains, berries, fruits, nuts, etc., in a plate. Try to pick these food items using a spoon, a toothpick or a pair of sticks. Fill the table below with appropriate tool(s) for picking each of these food items.

Bird Diary — Go near a tree. Close your eyes and listen to the sounds of different birds. Now open your eyes and focus on one bird at a time. Observe how it moves, what it eats, and where it sits. Write your observations in a Bird Diary! For each bird, note the following: Head colour, Back colour, Wing colours, Beak shape, Throat colour, Colour of legs, Interesting Facts.
List the animals which live in and around water bodies. Fill in the table given below.
During the visit to the nature trail, students collected leaves from different plants. Help them identify the plants based on the pictures of the leaves given below.
Observe the leaves of different plants in your neighbourhood and fill the table.

Leaf Autograph — Place a leaf on a notebook with the rough, vein-side facing up. Cover it with a plain sheet of paper. Rub a crayon over the paper, and watch the leaf's shape appear like magic! Try this with different leaves and see how each one has its own unique pattern. Name the plant or tree to which the leaf belongs.

sections.title: Tiny Animals
sections.content_summary: Students encounter insects in the forest, introduced through a grasshopper. Key features of insects are described: three pairs of legs, one pair of antennae, and sometimes two pairs of wings (as in flies and grasshoppers). Common forest insects named include ants, beetles, bees, flies, praying mantises, and butterflies. Students are asked to connect insect observations to their own home and neighbourhood environments.

tasks.task_text: What are the insects that you find at your home and in your surroundings?
Name the insects shown below.

sections.title: Let us Play the Game 'Web of Life'
sections.content_summary: Students play a yarn-based 'Web of Life' game in which each player represents a plant, animal, or natural element and passes yarn to represent connections and dependencies. Removing a player from the web shows how the collapse of one species or element ripples through the entire system. This experiential activity builds understanding of ecological interdependence and the concept of Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam — the world as one family.

sections.title: Let us reflect
sections.content_summary: A multi-part reflection section that consolidates learning through a variety of formats: imaginative writing, discussion on interdependence, a word-search puzzle on forest organisms (tiger, sparrow, egret, banyan, elephant, deer, snake, squirrel, kingfisher), a challenge to plan a nature walk, a role-play activity, origami paper-turtle making using recycled newspaper, and a find-out task about state symbols.

tasks.task_text: Which animal would you love to be? What special abilities would it give you?
How do you think different plants and animals are dependent on each other?
What is the role of a nature scientist?
Solve the puzzle. Find the treasures of the forest.

Take on the challenge: Plan a nature walk with your family or friends in a nearby park. To prepare yourself as a guide, identify few plants and animals, and study their features and interesting facts which you can explain during the walk.

Do an activity: Role-play: Create a scene of a forest in your classroom where students can perform roles of plants and animals, and of visitors. While enacting the play, focus on our role to protect animals and plants.
Let us make a paper turtle! Try using an old newspaper or used paper.
Write the name of your state animal, bird, and plant.

dual_strand.natural: Diversity of plants and animals and their unique adaptive features
dual_strand.human_cultural: Conservation responsibility, safety rules in nature, and ecological interdependence (Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam)
